item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not Keter because people quibble with that classification, and classifications aren't important to me. People almost never quibble with Euclid :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is this not Keter? It's uncontained, the Foundation doesn't know what causes it, and they don't know how many cases there could be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, weirdly enough I think its the title of this that makes it for me. Mostly because without the title, I would be like "but just… is that it?" and the title serves as a reminder that "yes, that is all. What more do you what? A mouse trap for the fingers?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, that was completely horrifying.

+1

item.title: STAFF POST - OPEN Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Show Reasoning
Hide Reasoning

The following crosslink proposal is being made based on the anomalies thematic relation to other sleep based horror articles on the authors suggestion.

Editing this:

Foundation front companies are to conduct "sleep studies" in an attempt to observe and document SCP-2365.

To this:

Foundation front companies are to conduct "sleep studies" in an attempt to observe and document SCP-2365.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welp, I don't want to necropost, but I feel obligated to link SCP-3966 for anyone reading this thread in the far future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's definitely simplistic by design, so if that's not working for you, I can hardly blame you! I was quite interested in the technical writing challenge of something so basic, which was a good exercise. I would encourage anyone with more than a few articles to their name to try writing something this short, just for the practice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm gonna be honest, I don't really care for this one. It seems overly simplistic.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree, too. I'm not sure what it is, but I prefer stories that are longer and more descriptive, but that's just me, I'm weird like that. Good story, just not a fan.

item.title: Headcanon
item.content:encoded: If you die in the dream you die in real life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that it's the name and series 1 feeling that attracts us. It's short and sweet, no long backstory, it's just there, and while that wouldn't be enough by itself, its name comes and rescues it. "The universe is trying to tell you that it hates you"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Frankly, I think this is fine as-is… no word-cruft for the sake of "setting";

Much as I would hate to change your upvote, I would note that there is quite a bit of word-cruft in this skip, almost all of it for the sake of setting. The first three paragraphs of the Description could probably be boiled down to three sentences - I deliberately dragged out the length to allow the reader to visualise the image as it appeared. I think that adds to the atmosphere and creepiness in a way that a direct description would not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Story isn't essential, but it does help provide more meat onto an article. Something bare-bones like this - as noted before, something that feels like the original SCP - only works if the anomaly itself is interesting enough and provokes enough thought.

This is just…bear traps over dreamers. OK, great, and? There's no emotional reaction there - it doesn't have to be horror or fear (Cassie and 999 are great examples) but it has to stir something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't really sure what to make of this one when I first read it a while back, but re-reading it now I find it's grown on me. It's very simple and there isn't much in the way of a story, but the sheer weirdness of this, and especially the implied idea that this might be a very common occurrence that happens to a lot/most/all of us at some point in our lives, is downright terrifying.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It can work, and in this case got a +1 from me, but it needs to be done perfectly (or nearly so) to shine.

This provides enough detail that the reader can fill in whatever story they want to - like the above comment about a latent reality bender. Put another way, less can be more, but you have to avoid the pitfall of "less is less".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Series III, for me, has become …

The sentiment expressed in that paragraph above (posted elsewhere, of course) what I was thinking of when citing Kalinin in my author post.

For the record, the other inspirations cited were:

  • Djoric - in particular SCP-2915, which was somewhat of a model in terms of the tone I was aiming for (especially in the earlier drafts with non-sequitur objects left behind).
  • Drewbear - who is an absolute specialist at this type of short-form, "object-only" skip. There are lots of examples, but my favourite is SCP-2627.
  • ophite - whose explanation for SCP-1193 (you can find it both in the discussion page for that skip and on ophite's author page) was another big influence: principally the idea that something can be so contrary to our understanding of ontology that we start to feel that maybe we, the Foundation/reader, are wrong about the universe and how it works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's getting upvotes because there are people who read it and enjoyed it. It's not that difficult. There's even folks upthread explaining their reasoning. I've never understood commentary like this.

In my case, I enjoyed it because it threaded the needle between "jarring and surreal imagery that evokes a definite feeling in the reader" and "lolrandom wackiness that happens because reasons." I can totally understand why some readers might not like that. I'm not standing here dumbfounded at how they could possibly come to a different conclusion than mine.

I've also been wanting to see more articles of a shorter nature. Series III, for me, has become characterized by bloated, inelegant text walls replete with exceedingly convenient notes left all over the place and half-realized characters who I'm supposed to care ever so deeply about. I'm all in favor of deep, engrossing narrative, and I've written quite a few long pieces myself, but I feel like the default expectation is some 5,000 word document explaining the convoluted backstory of a Thing That Does a Thing because backstory makes everything good for some reason. I supported this piece not only because I liked it, but because I'd like to see more successfully-executed short form works in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Almost every article gets an unvote from someone, even if only one. Some of us like that it's minimalist and not bogged down by a bunch of "we don't know why"s, which more than a few recent articles have done. Unknowns should be the assumption, not the exception. This is an organization whose main goal is the preservation of a naive ideal of "normality" for the general populous. I feel like the fiction should reflect that, rather than making the Foundation out to be semi-omniscient know-it-alls who have the answer more often than not. It's also been a while since I've seen an item with an interesting ability that can't be contained just by locking it up or making people ignore it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Frankly, I think this is fine as-is. No attempts at explanation (thereby making it non-anomalous, or at least predictable); no word-cruft for the sake of "setting"; no solid, reliable method of proper containment other than post-event cleanup. This is a solid, if minimalist, example of what a Euclid article should look like. The lack of a pattern of any kind is what really got me interested.

As for it being short, I really hate it when half of an article's sentences are "The cause of this is currently unknown." Stuff like that feels like padding to me. I think if it's not written down, the implication should be that it's unknown.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with the name "Dreamcatcher" being the title best to use.

Suppose a very young child heard the term "dream catcher" out of context, and then refused to go to sleep, because THIS is what they imagined it meant.

Suppose that child was a latent reality bender.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That could show the truly arbitrary nature of the SCP or reveal a pattern we (the out-of-universe readers) may discern but not the in-universe readers.

I'm not sure I understand how this would improve the article.

If I was to add a paragraph which set out the data on which the Foundation had concluded that the phenomenon's appearance was arbitrary, then I seem to be adding extra words without information that goes beyond the current final sentence.

If instead I set up a pattern discernible to the reader, then (apart from the risk that it makes the Foundation seem a bit dim) that undermines one of the central aims of this article. So many skips (mine included) have a pattern or underlying meaning which can be discovered or intuited, at least by the reader. Even if the patterns aren't known to current science, their mere existence suggests a universe that follows some rules or order. I didn't want this to be another one of those skips. This anomaly is intended to challenge the Foundation on an incredibly fundamental level - it doesn't have a pattern to its effect, no reason or intelligence exists behind it, so the idea of a rules-governed universe starts to fall apart. Is there even any point to the Foundation in a universe where this can, and does, happen?

So even when I included the following paragraph at the end of one of my drafts, I chose the objects deliberately in an attempt to avoid any pattern or conclusion being drawn:

In some SCP-QQQQ cases, foreign objects have been found in wounds, including:

  • sand
  • dental floss
  • a live grasshopper

However, I'm interested in opinions - for anyone who downvoted this skip, would the inclusion of the above (or something like it) change your vote? (And for anyone who upvoted, would it make you downvote?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps some good tales will come out of it, but it's not moving me.

I can completely understand if this isn't having its intended effect. Clearly, if a reader is not creeped out by the concept, there isn't much else here to upvote (unless anyone here upvotes on technical merit, which I will lay some claim to here).

I am intrigued by the idea that this could generate Tales. I can't imagine what more can be said on the subject, to be honest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the story is just "Here's a weird thing that happens at random forever and ever, amen."

I agree with this - that is what this skip is designed to be. I wanted to test the idea that story is necessary for a modern skip, and see whether writing purely for effect, and without narrative, could work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am of the opinion that the people affected can be worth exploring. I mean, I know it is stated to have no notable pattern. But there would still likely be an attempt to identify it. That could show the truly arbitrary nature of the SCP or reveal a pattern we (the out-of-universe readers) may discern but not the in-universe readers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm on the fence (well, slightly on the downvote side) with this one. At its core, it reminds me a great bit of Kate's cursed bed. The casual tone and ultimate violence, on the other hand, reminds me of one FaminePulse scip.

It's… eh. Sure it can be interesting when one thinks about it some more, but at first impressions its a phenomenon which randomly hurts or kills people for no good reason. A well written but charmless Series I style, if you will. Perhaps some good tales will come out of it, but it's not moving me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with the other folks. Sure, it's definitely creepy, but the story is just "Here's a weird thing that happens at random forever and ever, amen."

That's not even a campfire story.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anything but bear traps 3:

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like I said in a previous comment- it's short and simplistic, but it still manages to creep me out.

The name is a nice bonus, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is quirky-weird in the best sense.

Upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get why this is getting upvotes. There's honestly not that much to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gotta admit it, I was a bit skeptical after reading it. I overthought it as a "old-fashioned" article, but it was not enough for me to meh-vote…

Then I checked for the name…

Then I upboated.

It's okay if you're looking for a new name, but this one nailed it perfectly. Seriously. And while this may sound asshole-ish (and I swear, that is not my intention at all), I will revert my vote if you change the title1

Footnotes
1. This is NOT a threat. The author has the right to change the title as They desires, but that'd mean the piece that made this work for me would no longer be there. However, I can change my mind if any possible new name proves itself as worthy as the current one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short article that left me feeling a little creeped out by its implications. While I can understand why others might find this lacking in substance, I think anything else added to this article would only bog it down.

+1. I dig it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel the same way, except that despite all that, I still find myself deeply creeped out.
Which means I upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed. It's written well I think, but there's nothing that really grabs me.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm at a loss as to what the draw is. It seems perfunctory and not terribly interesting, the sort of article which is posted and deleted on a daily basis around here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. It makes me laugh, which (probably) makes me a bad person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Incidentally, today I woke up and immediately in the dregs of dream-to-waking felt like listening1 to Jack Johnson's "Better Together".

But if all of these dreams
Might find their way into my day-to-day scene
I'd be under the impression
I was somewhere in between…

Lo and behold, this SCP was posted. Funny thing, that?

+1

Footnotes
1. Anyone else ever get cravings for music similar to cravings for certain foods?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i personally am for "Dreamcatcher" since well i came up with that one and i'm clearly biased

"To sleep, perchance to dream" is the name of a tale on the site, incidentally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only complaint I have is that I've had another idea germinating in my mind for much of the past year. It was a slow, creeping article, starting with an anomalous protein and using hard science to build it into an unsettling implication about commonplace phenomena that occur during sleep. It was essentially going to be a series of experiments that function like an exploration log that never leaves the laboratory.

Then this comes along and presents that creep in just over 300 words.

Excellent job, psul. +1 all the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short, simple and quite great article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahhh, I see the direction you were going now. I can also see why it seemed more tacked on for you but felt more in place for me.
When I first read it it seemed like nightmares were leaking through (drowning in sand, plague of grasshoppers) that might cause a person to wake up in a start and activate it. Whatever was last on their mind came out of the wounds because of some unknown nature involving the dreams and their connection to the trap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do wish it was a bit longer

I would be interested in discussing that thought, if you'd like. When I get around to writing about this on my author page, I will have quite a lot to say about the decisions made in terms of length and structure, but suffice to say that I decided early on to keep it very short. Is there something in particular that you think could be added?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
things sometimes being left in the wounds like the persons dreams were leaking through them

Wow - you know, I'd never thought about those objects like that. When the draft still included the appearance of those objects, my headcanon was that this was an attempt by a completely alien intelligence to communicate with us - unfortunately it had no idea how to do so, hence some of the other title options.

I really like your theory though, and it almost makes me want to reinstate the relevant paragraph. The reason I decided to leave it out is that it changes the tone by adding too much "weird" to the "creepy", and would therefore feel tacked on (which it was).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"R.I.P. Van Winkle"

That is fantastic, as is "You're gonna feel that…" - another possible angle for the title was something around "a face to wake up next to".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do wish it was a bit longer, but it's weird and awful and I really like it. Nice work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved it in drafts and I love it now. I do miss the things sometimes being left in the wounds like the persons dreams were leaking through them but it works perfectly fine without it.

+1

For a name, there is always a version of "What Dreams May Come".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short, sweet, and to the point. I'm torn between the Hamlet reference and Dreamcatcher. Putting up the suggestions of "Dream a little dream of Me", "Preludes and Nocturnes" and "R.I.P. Van Winkle".

ETA: "You're gonna feel that one in the morning!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good to see this up. Sweet, simple and doesn't overstay its welcome, although the implications are obvious and a little disturbing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After writing a lot in a row for On Mount Golgotha, this felt like a nice palate cleanser. I hope you enjoy - at least this isn't one of those SCPs that is so long that it makes you tired.

I have run through so many possible titles for this one, including "To sleep, perchance to dream", "On the Impossibility of Communication in cases of Fundamentally Different Understandings of Ontology", "The search for meaning is itself meaningless", "Dreamcatcher" - if anyone comes up with a suggestion, I am open to changing it on the Series III list.

As usual, much thanks is due. So many people read this through (in various forms) and gave helpful comments and feedback. Thank you to Thetataurus, sirpudding, Shaggydredlocks, Zyn, WrongJohnSilver, Varren Erelim, LurkD, A Random Day, BIDZ180, Aiden Eldritch and Decibelle.

This also had a number of key influences, including writings for this site by Kalinin, ophite, Djoric and Drewbear.
